items.title: AC verify report uses literal \n instead of newlines
items.intent: AC verify report must render with real newlines so validator receives properly formatted output
items.description:
run_ac_verification() builds the report string using '
' in bash double-quoted strings (e.g. report="${report}  PASS: $ac_text
"). In bash, '
' inside double quotes is a literal backslash-n, not a newline. The garbled report is injected into validator prompts and retry context, degrading AI quality.
steps: In .aishore/aishore, find run_ac_verification() (around line 1218)
Locate all lines building the report variable with string concatenation using \\n
Replace \\"\\n\\" inside double-quoted strings with $\x27\\n\x27 (ANSI-C quoting) or use printf to append newlines
Verify the fix does not affect other uses of the report variable
Run a sprint with AC verify commands and confirm report output contains real newlines
acceptanceCriteria: AC verify report passed to validator contains actual newlines (not literal \\n)
Retry prompt with AC failure context displays properly formatted multi-line output
items.groomingNotes: Confirmed: bash '\n' in double-quoted strings does not produce newlines. Fix is straightforward — change to $'\n' or printf. Low risk, small scope.

items.title: Merge conflict breaks multi-sprint loop without logging skipped items
items.intent: When a merge conflict aborts a multi-sprint run, the output must clearly list which items were not attempted
items.description: In cmd_run(), when _handle_sprint_success() returns non-zero (merge conflict), the loop breaks immediately. Items queued but not yet attempted are silently abandoned. The batch summary only covers items that were started. This makes it hard to know what needs to be re-run after resolving the conflict.
steps: In .aishore/aishore, find the main sprint loop in cmd_run() that calls _handle_sprint_success()
Before the loop, build an ordered list of all items that will be attempted in this batch
On break (merge conflict), compute the set difference: all queued items minus started items
Log each not-attempted item ID with a clear message before breaking
Ensure print_batch_summary or a post-loop block surfaces the skipped items count and IDs
Test with two queued items where the first causes a merge conflict
acceptanceCriteria: After a merge conflict aborts a multi-sprint run, output lists item IDs that were queued but not started
Batch summary accurately reflects items not attempted due to early abort
items.groomingNotes: Real UX gap: users lose track of which items were skipped after a merge conflict abort. Needs pre-loop item list and post-abort diff reporting.

items.title: Dead if/else branch in AC JSON construction is misleading
items.intent: AC JSON construction code must not have dead branches that confuse maintainers
items.description: In cmd_backlog_edit(), the AC entry builder has an if/else that checks whether an entry starts with '{' (JSON object) vs plain string, but both branches execute the identical statement: ac_json="$ac_json$entry". The distinction is never acted upon. This is dead/misleading code that could hide future bugs if someone adds logic to only one branch.
steps: In .aishore/aishore, find the AC JSON construction block in cmd_backlog_edit() (around line 3688)
Identify the if/else that checks whether an entry starts with "{" — both branches do the same thing
Remove the if/else and keep just the single statement: ac_json="$ac_json$entry"
Verify that backlog edit --ac and --ac-verify still produce valid JSON items
Run .aishore/aishore backlog check on an item with AC entries to confirm no regression
acceptanceCriteria: The if/else branch is removed, only the single common statement remains
backlog edit --ac and --ac-verify still produce valid JSON items
backlog check on an item with AC entries shows no regression
items.groomingNotes: Tech debt: dead if/else branches mislead future developers. Trivial fix — remove the conditional.

items.title: AC verify command output truncated at 1000 bytes, may cut mid-character
items.intent: AC verification failure output must be complete enough to diagnose the problem
items.description: run_ac_verification() uses 'head -c 1000' to cap output from AC verify commands. This can cut multi-byte UTF-8 characters in half, producing garbled text. Even for ASCII, 1000 bytes is often too small for test output. The truncated output is injected into prompts, where incomplete stack traces or error messages reduce the AI's ability to fix issues.
steps: In .aishore/aishore, find run_ac_verification() where it uses head -c 1000 to cap output
Replace byte-based truncation (head -c 1000) with line-based truncation (e.g. tail -n 50 or head -n 50)
Or use a higher byte limit (e.g. 5000) that is unlikely to split useful error output
Consider adding a truncation indicator (e.g. "[... output truncated]") so the AI knows output was cut
Verify AC verify failure output is readable in retry prompts after the change
acceptanceCriteria: AC verify output is not split mid-character (use line-based truncation instead of byte truncation)
Truncation limit is configurable or set to a value adequate for typical test output (e.g., 3000 chars)
items.groomingNotes: PO review: bumped to should. Truncated error output directly hurts sprint recovery — the AI has less signal to fix failures. More failures = less value delivered. 1000 byte limit is too small for real test output and the byte-split-char bug is a correctness issue.

items.title: config.yaml silently ignored when yq unavailable and no warning shown
items.intent: Users must be clearly warned when their config.yaml changes are being silently ignored due to missing yq
items.description: load_config() falls back to grep-based parsing when yq is not installed. This fallback is much less reliable and can silently ignore valid configuration. The warning is only triggered if uncommented settings are detected by grep. A config file that uses valid YAML but is structured in a way grep misses will be silently ignored, leaving the user thinking their overrides are active when they are not.
steps: In .aishore/aishore, find load_config() where yq availability is checked (around line 267)
Add an early check: if config.yaml exists and has non-empty content, and yq is not available, always emit a warning
Warning should name the config file, explain what is being ignored, and give install instructions for yq
Ensure the warning is shown regardless of whether grep finds any settings
Test by creating a config.yaml with a setting and running aishore without yq installed
acceptanceCriteria: When yq is absent and config.yaml exists with content, a clear warning is always shown regardless of grep detection
Warning text explains what yq is and how to install it
items.groomingNotes: PO re-review: bumped to must. Silent config failure on first run destroys new-user trust at exactly the moment they're evaluating the tool. If yq isn't installed (common), every override they set is a lie. No other item has this failure mode — the user did everything right and gets wrong behavior with no signal. Must fix before new user onboarding can be considered reliable.

items.title: backlog add and edit have no --step flag for setting implementation steps
items.intent: Users must be able to add and replace implementation steps via CLI without hand-editing JSON — steps are required for sprint readiness and a missing flag forces a manual workaround.
items.description: backlog add creates items with steps: [] and backlog edit has no flag to append or replace steps. The only way to populate the steps array is to edit the backlog JSON directly, which violates the CLI-first design. Both commands already follow a consistent flag pattern (--ac for AC entries, --scope for scope patterns). Steps should follow the same pattern: --step adds one step (repeatable), --clear-steps resets the array. This unblocks grooming items without requiring JSON editing.
acceptanceCriteria: backlog add --step 'Do X' creates an item with steps: ["Do X"]
backlog add --step 'Do X' --step 'Do Y' creates steps in order
backlog edit <ID> --step 'Do X' appends X to the existing steps array
backlog edit <ID> --clear-steps resets steps to empty array
items.groomingNotes: New item added during groom session. Implementation pattern mirrors --ac/--scope: local steps array, --step appends one entry per flag call, --clear-steps resets. In cmd_backlog_add(): add local steps_entries=() array, --step flag appends to it, build steps_json from the array, pass --argjson steps_arr to add_item, set steps: $steps_arr (replacing the hardcoded steps: []). In cmd_backlog_edit(): add --step flag that accumulates entries same as --ac, build steps JSON, use jq_updates += '| .steps = ((.steps // []) + $newsteps)'. Add --clear-steps: jq_updates += '| .steps = []'. --ready flag warning about no steps is expected (bootstrapping issue).

items.title: backlog show renders AC {text, verify} objects as raw JSON instead of plain text
items.intent: backlog show must display acceptance criteria as readable text — when entries are {text, verify} objects, only the text should be shown, not the JSON structure.
items.description: When backlog edit --ac 'Some check' --ac-verify 'make test' is used, the AC entry is stored as a JSON object {"text": "Some check", "verify": "make test"}. In cmd_backlog_show(), the jq display expression at line ~3720 uses (.acceptanceCriteria // [] | .[] | "  - \(.)"), which calls tojson on the entire object, producing output like: '  - {"text":"Some check","verify":"make test"}'. The fix is to check entry type: if it's a string, show it as-is; if it's an object, extract .text (and optionally show the verify command in a shorter form).
acceptanceCriteria: backlog show <ID> displays plain-string AC entries unchanged
backlog show <ID> displays {text, verify} AC entries as the .text value only (not raw JSON)
backlog show <ID> optionally shows verify command on same or next line in a readable format
items.groomingNotes: Fix location: cmd_backlog_show() jq expression around line 3720. Current: (.acceptanceCriteria // [] | .[] | "  - \(.)"). Fix: (.acceptanceCriteria // [] | .[] | "  - \(if type == "object" then .text + (if .verify then " [verify: \(.verify)]" else "" end) else . end)"). Run: backlog add with --ac 'plain' --ac 'text' --ac-verify 'cmd' then backlog show to confirm. This is a display-only fix — no data changes.

items.title: VALIDATE_CMD output injected untruncated into retry prompts
items.intent: Retry prompts must not contain unbounded validation output — token bloat degrades AI fix quality and can push critical context out of attention.
items.description: In _run_retry_loop(), validate_output is captured with 'cat $validate_tmpfile' (no truncation limit) and injected raw into retry_extra_prompt. run_ac_verification() and the pre-flight check both apply OUTPUT_TRUNCATE_LINES truncation, but the VALIDATE_CMD retry path does not. For verbose test suites (cargo test, pytest, mocha), this produces thousands of lines in the retry prompt, degrading AI quality. The validator-agent retry path at line ~1769 has the same issue.
steps: In .aishore/aishore, find _run_retry_loop() and locate the validate_output capture: validate_output=$(cat "$validate_tmpfile" 2>/dev/null || true)

Replace the cat with the same truncation pattern used in run_ac_verification(): capture wc -l, use tail -n $OUTPUT_TRUNCATE_LINES, prepend '[... output truncated, showing last N of M lines]' if truncated
Verify the validator-agent retry path also uses the truncated validate_output (line ~1769)
Confirm pre-flight check already has truncation (it does, around line 1650); no change needed there
acceptanceCriteria: VALIDATE_CMD output injected into retry prompts is truncated to OUTPUT_TRUNCATE_LINES lines
When output is truncated, the retry prompt includes a '[... output truncated, showing last N of M lines]' indicator
Truncation matches the pattern used in run_ac_verification() for consistency
items.groomingNotes: Same pattern as BUG-004 (AC verify truncation) but missed for the VALIDATE_CMD retry path. Fix is additive — apply OUTPUT_TRUNCATE_LINES truncation at one spot in _run_retry_loop(). Low risk, clear scope.

items.title: backlog edit has no --clear-ac flag to reset acceptance criteria
items.intent: Users must be able to reset AC via CLI — without --clear-ac, wrong AC entries require hand-editing JSON, violating the CLI-first design principle.
items.description: backlog edit --step has a companion --clear-steps flag; backlog edit --scope has a companion --clear-scope flag. But --ac has no --clear-ac. Once AC entries are added, the only way to remove them is to hand-edit the backlog JSON. This is inconsistent with the established pattern and violates CLI-first design. Fix: add --clear-ac to cmd_backlog_edit() that resets acceptanceCriteria to [], following the same pattern as --clear-steps.
steps: In .aishore/aishore, find cmd_backlog_edit() and locate the --clear-steps case as reference (around line 3843-3846)
Add a --clear-ac case: set jq_updates with .acceptanceCriteria = [] and update update_desc
Add --clear-ac documentation to the help text in cmd_backlog_edit error message and cmd_help
Test: backlog edit BUG-003 --clear-ac (verify AC resets), then backlog edit BUG-003 --ac 'New' (verify fresh entry appears)
acceptanceCriteria: backlog edit <ID> --clear-ac resets acceptanceCriteria to []
backlog edit <ID> --clear-ac --ac 'New AC' results in only the new entry present
--clear-ac appears in the backlog edit help output
items.groomingNotes: Clear pattern mismatch — --clear-steps and --clear-scope both exist, --clear-ac does not. Fix is additive, one case block in cmd_backlog_edit plus help text update. Low risk.

items.title: remove_items_by_status takes full path while other CRUD helpers take relative filename
items.intent: Backlog CRUD helpers must use a consistent path convention — the current asymmetry is a latent footgun that will cause silent wrong-file operations when new callers are added.
items.description: update_item() and remove_item() accept a relative filename (e.g. 'backlog.json') and prepend BACKLOG_DIR internally. remove_items_by_status() takes a full path directly. Callers today are consistent, but the asymmetry is not documented and will cause bugs when someone adds a new call site with the wrong convention. Fix: change remove_items_by_status to accept a relative filename and prepend BACKLOG_DIR internally, matching the established pattern.
steps: In .aishore/aishore, find remove_items_by_status() around line 703 and update it to accept a relative filename like update_item() and remove_item()
Change the jq command argument from '$file' to '$BACKLOG_DIR/$file'
Update cmd_clean() callers (line ~4025-4026) to pass just the filename ('backlog.json', 'bugs.json') instead of the full path variables
Run .aishore/aishore clean --dry-run and then clean on a test setup to confirm no regression
acceptanceCriteria: remove_items_by_status accepts a relative filename consistent with update_item and remove_item
cmd_clean still correctly removes done items after the refactor
grep confirms no other callers of remove_items_by_status exist with full paths
items.groomingNotes: Tech debt: path convention inconsistency between remove_items_by_status and the other CRUD helpers. Small refactor with one call site to update. Low risk — only one caller exists today (cmd_clean).
